help.description: PXE netboot HTTP-fetch macros (drive iPXE's kernel/initrd GETs by hand)

get.description: Request line: GET {{PATH}} HTTP/1.1 (set PATH first)

head-kernel.description: HEAD {{KERNEL}} — headers only, no body (confirm size fast)

close.description: Connection: close (server closes after this response)

end.description: End headers — send the blank line that triggers the response

demo-bootipxe.description: Demo: fetch /boot.ipxe — the script iPXE chainloads

steps.display: ═══ Fetch boot.ipxe (what iPXE runs after it loads) ═══
iPXE's embedded script usually does `chain http://.../boot.ipxe`.
Here we pull that script the same way, over plain HTTP.
Request line  (//get-bootipxe)
Host header  (//host)
Connection: close  (//close)
Blank line ends the request  (//end)
═══ The body above IS your boot.ipxe — note its kernel/initrd URLs ═══

demo-kernel.description: Demo: full GET of the kernel — request + response headers

steps.display: ═══ GET the kernel ({{KERNEL}}) ═══
This is exactly iPXE's `kernel http://{{HOST}}{{KERNEL}} ...` fetch.
Request line  (//get-kernel)
Host header  (//host)
User-Agent  (//ua)
Connection: close  (//close)
Blank line  (//end) — watch for 200 OK + Content-Length
═══ 200 OK + Content-Length = the kernel iPXE would boot ═══
(The binary body streams below; use //head-kernel to see headers only.)

demo-chain.description: Demo: the whole iPXE HTTP sequence — boot.ipxe → kernel → initrd

steps.display: ═══ The full HTTP side of a PXE boot ═══
Reconnect (Connection: close drops the socket between requests).
Step 1/3 — GET /boot.ipxe (the script)
Step 2/3 — GET {{KERNEL}} (the kernel named in that script)
Step 3/3 — GET {{INITRD}} (the initramfs)
═══ Three 200s = everything iPXE needs is being served correctly ═══
